summary: Charity Majors argues that the tech industry's obsession with '10x engineers' is misguided because individual engineers don't own software—teams do. She makes the case that truly great engineering organizations are ones where normal, workaday engineers can consistently ship code and have business impact, rather than orgs that depend on a few brilliant individuals. The path to high performance is building better sociotechnical systems (fast deploys, observability, guard rails) that enable normal people to do great work.
key_points: Individual productivity measurements are flawed because skills are context-dependent and non-transferable across domains
Teams own software, not individuals—the smallest unit of software ownership is the engineering team
Great engineering orgs enable normal engineers to move fast and have impact, rather than requiring brilliance to get anything done
Deploy speed is the central feedback loop—short deploy cycles reduce cognitive load and coordination costs
Companies overindex on hiring pre-minted talent rather than building environments that develop engineers
Diverse, mixed-level teams are more resilient than monocultures of senior engineers
money_quotes: The smallest unit of software ownership and delivery is the engineering team.

Any asshole can build an org where the most experienced, brilliant engineers in the world can build product and make progress. That is not hard. And putting all the spotlight on individual ability has a way of letting your leaders off the hook for doing their jobs.
Great engineers are made, not born.
When your systems are designed to be used by normal engineers, all that excess brilliance they have can get poured into the product itself, instead of wasting it on navigating the system itself.
Software engineering isn't about writing lots of lines of code, it's about solving business problems using technology.
key_insight: The mark of a great engineering organization isn't hiring the most brilliant individuals, but building sociotechnical systems where normal engineers can consistently ship, learn, and move the business forward.
